* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. logs.name: flat-round
logs.text: It is, in that case, up to the representative to decide whether it is better for the people to receive their mail on Sundays or not, and it should be the job of the representative to know whether his/her constituency wants mail on Sundays.

logs.name: frosty-proton
logs.text:
For many issues, I agree. The constituents don't know enough to make informed opinions, so we hire an expert to get informed and make decisions on our behalf. No problem there.

But, for some issues there is no objectively right or wrong answer that can be identified as the choice that's "in our best interests." The example I like to use for this is Sunday mail delivery by the Post Office. It costs the Post Office money. If it were a profit generator, then the decision would be easy, but it's not. It's a money loser. But that doesn't necessarily tell us what to do because it might be something the people are willing to spend money on. It's a convenience and we may or may not think it's worth the price.

We've got something the government isn't constitutionally required to provide, nor is it service prohibited by the government. It's something nice to have, but isn't really necessary, and it costs us money, but it's a small amount we can easily afford. In this scenario, why shouldn't the politician represent their constituents' wishes instead of their "best interests?" Let some polling decide the matter and vote whichever way people are leaning.

logs.name: livid-balance
logs.text: Seriously?! You think it's up to the representative to decide if people want Sunday mail? That's ridiculous. We pay them to represent us, not to cater to whims. It's a waste of taxpayer money and shows a complete lack of accountability. @frosty-proton you're missing the point here. It's not about "best interests," it's about basic service. And it costs money! I'm absolutely fed up with this kind of thinking.
